story:
A young voice answers, “You’ve reached nine-five-eight-seven-six. I’m Beth.”

Karen, a medical coordinator, says, “May I speak with Ms. Exbeth?”

The girl chuckles. “Mr. Ex? Like a pirate who signs with an X?” She calls, “Mom, a Karen wants you,” and hands the phone over.

“This is Bessie Hadick,” the woman says. “No one named Exbeth here. They’re adding a new phone prefix in this part of the state. Maybe you’ve got the wrong area.”

Karen hangs up, unsettled.

At work, Karen tells Emma, her supervisor, that the call made no sense. Emma says Exbeth May Hadick—an aging writer—has been peculiar for years and is overdue for a visit. Karen can go the next afternoon but has to bring a colleague, Mike Mitchum, because it’s a yearly update. Mike, Emma adds, is the only person Exbeth seems to like.

Outside the next day, Karen meets Mike and is surprised by his sleek wheelchair and custom van. As Mike drives, Karen says she’s heard Sunset Street is supposed to be dangerous. He nods, then, as they turn onto a pretty stretch where the fronts of the houses bloom, he shrugs.

“Exbeth pays her gardener to keep the whole street’s curb neat,” Mike says. “Only the fronts. The backs are a mess.”

At the flower-filled house, Karen knocks and rings. No answer. Mike raps with his cane, then wrinkles his nose. “Something’s off,” he says. “The key’s taped behind the bird nesting box.”

Karen finds the key, unlocks, pushes the door, and screams. Stench. Blood. A severed hand. Mike startles, jerks his chair backward, catches a wheel on the edge, and tips off the stoop, hitting the walk—shaken and humiliated.

Karen snaps into command. She calls 911, identifies herself, reports a possible homicide, and orders Mike not to move. She cushions his head with her jacket and stays with him until the ambulance arrives. An officer—Benjamin—separates her from the doorway and asks for her legal name.

“Katreena Whitaker Merlin,” she says, and walks him through what happened.

That night, her son Bobby complains he’s starving; her daughter Clare tells him to hush—Mom was on the news. Karen corrects her: she didn’t see a murder; she found a murdered woman. Work calls. She gets ten days off to recover. She proposes a week at their mountain cabin. They cheer. Dinner is sushi at Tokyo Su, which also serves hamburgers, so both kids win.

Back at work, Emma calls Karen lucky for the timing of her leave. The news has pinned the killing on a disturbed neighbor whose yard backs onto Exbeth’s property. Mike asks Karen to meet with a detective—“a formality.”

Detective Knudson joins Karen and Mike in the conference room. He says call records show Karen phoned Exbeth’s line three days after the estimated time of death. Karen plays the office recording: the bright young voice introducing “nine-five-eight-seven-six,” the quick laugh—“Mr. Ex? Like a pirate?”—then, “Mom, a Karen wants you,” and the woman’s voice: “This is Bessie Hadick. No one named Exbeth here. They’re adding a new phone prefix…maybe you’ve got the wrong area.”

The detective plays a clip from an old interview; the voice matches—Exbeth’s, when young.

Karen pulls Exbeth’s last book from her tote, a collection of life stories she read during her leave. She’s highlighted a scene with the same odd exchange about the name and the X. The detective adds that the year Exbeth was thirteen, the region added phone prefixes, and her family’s voice-ID code back then was 95876.

They sit with it. The call came from “Beth” sounding thirteen; the words match the book’s memory; the number matches her teen voice ID—yet the timestamp says it happened after her death.

“Odd?” Karen says.

“It is.”
